speaker: interviewer
text: You're in the woods. You've separated from the other group. Where did you go the first night? Did you stay in the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We came in the morning to the first house we saw. And we went in. We asked the farmer if he wants to keep us. And we told him we can give him some money because we had valuables. I don't remember what.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we gave him money. And he agreed on that, to keep us over the day. Because, at night, we had to move again. So he agreed with us. And he kept us in the attic somewhere. And we were lucky because in the same place they searched all over. And they caught a lot of these people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we just were lucky. They didn't catch us. Because the whole area, they looked and looked because they knew people couldn't come. And as a matter of fact, we were only I think four kilometers away from camp with the whole night running away. So we were very near to it, very near.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many escaped, about 450?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- well, the camp in the time from uprising were around 600 people. But I don't think all escaped. It shows in the movie, but I'm not familiar-- I don't know it, really-- that a lot of people, all the people, just sat down and gave up. They didn't want to run, didn't want to do anything. Most of the younger people, I think, ran away. And I guess most of everybody who could run away ran away. Because it was just for everybody to run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now it's the next day. You leave the farm. Where do you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, now we leave in the evening, the farm. And we just kept inside. It was in the a rural area. So we were always either in the woods-- mostly in the woods because we knew it is not safe anywhere else.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- we ran at night in the woods. And surely you see everything. Selma, all the things she saw-- friends she saw dead, and she made me more scary as it was. So we ran till-- and we tried already, because I wanted to come someplace. Maybe some farmer will want to keep me. So we kept asking people if  they want to keep us for money. We told them-- well, surely mostly did not-- did not.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had-- sometimes we could leave during the day. We tried at night, during the day being hidden in someplace. But we couldn't always find a farmer or the place where to hide out. So we were sometimes just in the field. And one time, we were hiding in a-- in a-- hoy--

speaker: interviewer
text: Haystack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Haystack. Haystack. And then some kids were playing there. And they came on the haystack and saw us. So it was very dangerous. In the middle of the day, we had to go expose us and go in-- go in somewhere else.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one fact I remember, one thing, we were two farmers, one with wagons. Maybe Selma told it. And-- and I spoke Polish, so they-- oh, they said they will take us.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they didn't know that I speak Polish. So while we are on the wagon, I on one and she on the other, and they say, oh, the Jews, we will take them, and we do things like that. So I say to Selma, jump. And we jump right away on these wagons, and we ran away. So they didn't do anything. And we had more cases like that. And it was really hard because sometimes we will ask people for water, wouldn't give us nothing-- food, no, not at all. So we had-- we [INAUDIBLE] around about two weeks, something like that, till we went into a farmer, and we ask him if he wants to keep us. And we told him we have money we'd give him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, well, I would like to do it, but I cannot because I am too much visible here, too many neighbors. He said, my brother, what lives about 10 miles-- or kilometers away from here, he is very much isolated, and he might be willing to do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, so he put me, put us on the wagon. He dressed me up as a-- no, her. He dressed Selma up as an old lady. And we put on the long wagons, covered with branches. He drove us to his brother. Now, that was a very dangerous thing what he did. If they would have caught us, we all  would have been dead on the spot. And all his family would have. So it was a very big risk what the man did. We were lucky. He brought us to his brother, and the brother agreed to keep us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Novak. ?] The two brothers, [? Novak. ?] One is Adam, and the other is Piotr, I think. They both died. They both died already.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you and Selma stayed in touch with them for years and years?

speaker: interviewee
text: We still with family in touch. As a matter of fact, we just sent them money for the holidays. So we are in touch.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your time in hiding like, Chaim?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we always saw the good side of it. It was very hard and very bad, but it is a question of survival. So you don't ask the conditions. You ask, can I survive today, live? There was a day gained again, longer that you live.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as I say, we always were grateful to this, regardless of the conditions. Now, when we came, we gave them all we had, all money. I don't know what it was. It was, I assume, a lot-- diamonds, gold, whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- after a time, they thought we might still have some money. So they decided-- we slept on the attic. There were these cows were on the top. So he said, well, let's go, that we go where the porks are.

speaker: interviewer
text: The pigs?

speaker: interviewee
text: The pigs. I mixed the language. The pigs are. And-- what was it, he want to look if we have something hidden. Now, we didn't have any hidden. We were very honest with him. We gave him what we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that makes him feeling a little better. Selma already said we had the scabies, and we had a hard time. We-- you could use an ointment that it could help it, this phosphorite and something like that. But he didn't-- he didn't want to go to buy it or whatever. So I still had a gold fountain pen. So I said, take that, and go buy this ointment. So he did, and that helped us a lot. But that it very bad. We slept-- sometimes he brought us some to eat, and we kept, sometimes, a piece of bread under our pillow, and the rats came and took it away at night.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I say, taking the condition and the circumstances we were, the conditions really didn't count much. We ate, we had food, so much we could survive. And he had a toilet and [INAUDIBLE], what he came every day to pick up. So we were grateful for it, regardless of conditions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about Selma's pregnancy and how that upset the farmer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we surely didn't know this, but she said she thought that she is. So it was really a big problem. So-- but we got freed by the Russians before it is so far. So the day-- we were lucky, too. The same day when the Russian came, of a day before, one of his nephews or nieces, they came playing in this house, in their house. And at the once, they jumped up top where we were lying. And they saw us. And they were very surprised, but nobody knew about it. So we thought, no, that is the end of it. But I don't remember the day. After, or whatever, the Russians came, so we came down.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now this is 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was-- yeah, 19-- June or July 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Where did you go from the farm once you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we wanted to go away from the farm. We wanted to go to the city, because, first of all, we had to make a living, do something. So we went-- he brought us to Lublin, which was about 10 miles away. And we found some other Jews there. Now wait a minute. Did we go to-- no, we went to Chelm. Chelm was only a--

speaker: interviewer
text: A smaller town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And-- and then we found some other Jews. Other people came back. And there were people from this place, and they had their own houses before, for house. So we got a room somewhere to leave-- live. And there we were quite some time, because the child was born there. And I went traveling. I am traveling and-- to-- to-- to earn some money.

speaker: interviewer
text: You sold things like shoelaces?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I always went-- I always went to places where the Germans moved back. And where the Germans were was always some merchandise, still. So I bought it, and I brought it, and I sold it. But it took me sometimes two weeks to go back of one place because there wasn't already communication. I hitchhiked on the trains and wherever I could and traveled. And you drag it with you, whatever you get. And at one time, the police caught me, that I have merchandise. So they took me-- the whole thing away. And I don't know what happened, they gave me something back or not. Anyway, we somehow managed it. We had a hard time, but surely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you heard about a train. You heard about going back to Holland at this time. And you decided to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we heard-- we heard that the Dutch people can collect-- go to someplace, and they will be-- go back to Holland. So I said I am a Dutchman, too, and with Selma. And we went. Somehow we got in connection with them. And it was a Dutch captain. He knew the-- our story. And he wanted to cooperate with us. And he helped us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you pretended to be Dutch?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I didn't speak, I didn't go, things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like on the ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the ships, some of us-- first of all, we had-- the child died on the boat. They got a stomach poisoning. I guess-- there was not really much food. They had full milk, powdered milk, something, whatever, and they got a poisoning. And there was no medicine, nothing. And it died. And it was buried in the sea. That was in Greece. We were by [INAUDIBLE], the 'Agaceus' Sea-- how do you say, 'Agacea' Sea?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sea, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the name of it-- by Greece. 'Agace'--

speaker: interviewer
text: 'Agaceus?'

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the name of the sea-- I will look it up in English how it is. I can look it up anyway.

words.text: I can look it up anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then where did the boat land first?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the boat land-- we-- it landed in Marseilles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Marseilles.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Marseilles. She was seasick, terribly seasick. And she was on the  top of the boat, in the middle of the boat. It didn't help. And she was so sick. She was vomiting constantly. It was very bad. So-- but we were lucky we made it, because when the boat left, we were in Odessa. Oh, wait. We went Back to Holland on Odessa. When the boat left, it was one woman, which didn't go with the ship. She wanted all of us to go with the ship. But this one woman left us, very sad, a sad story. You know, she was older, pretending to be Dutch but got caught or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- so anyway, so we went from Odessa to-- to Marseilles. The boat went to Marseilles. I forgot the name of the boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you-- you had never been to Holland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We went-- No, before, not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Marseilles-- from Odessa-- from Marseilles we were-- we were-- by train we went to Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what was your reception like in Holland when you got to Selma's hotel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, for me it was not a reception because I wasn't a Dutchman. And for Selma, wasn't a bad reception. [INAUDIBLE], things like that. Selma can better tell that. So I liked to go to Holland because it is not-- it was not Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: You met your brother-in-law, your new brother-in-law.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I met-- met my brother-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you all lived together in what had been her--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there was a hotel. We lived together-- separate and together in the same place. And-- and we worked in the hotel for a time. And later, we couldn't get along too good. So we decided to go out. And we started to have a business partner in textiles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how long did you stay in Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till 1957.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had two more children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fred and Eleida.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what made you decide to go to Israel from Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had some bad experiences in Holland. I had this business together with somebody. And somehow, he swindled me. I lost almost everything. And so-- and in-- in Holland, you cannot start a business just to want to start. Like here, you start whatever you can, and you do. You have to have special license. And the reason-- the reason I started this business is because an uncle from Selma had a business, and the license was still good, but we could write it over. So we had written it over to our name. And the partner had a-- a location, a store. So we came together. He couldn't have the license. And I had a license.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we built together. And it ended up after so many years that we did very well. But it ended up that he swindled us. And we had a hard time to go back to business life. So we had to go, either go-- I wanted to go to America, and Selma wanted to go to Israel. So Selma won?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to Israel first.

speaker: interviewee
text: She won because we couldn't come into America. We didn't have anybody [INAUDIBLE]. So we-- we could easily go to Israel. So we went to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did you go to a kibbutz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we call it a [NON-ENGLISH]. What that is is that you work together but you live separate. In a kibbutz, you live together and you work together. In a [NON-ENGLISH], what means a village, a partner village. What that means, really, is, you have your own quarters where you live and your children with you and everything, but you go to work with this for the whole community. So-- but I couldn't stand it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't like it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't stand it from the first day on. So I already planned to do something. So I went every other week. I had two days free. Every week, you get a day free, so I went every other week and tried to get away from that. And after-- I don't remember how many months-- seven, eight, nine months, I found another place. And I wanted to start to work somewhere. And we moved away from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then you opened a-- some vegetable stores.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We bought some-- a woman went to America, and she sold it. And we bought it. We hesitated first to buy it in our village, a vegetable store. How can that exist? Well, anyway, we bought it and built it up. We had three more. And so we build up the business. And we made good money. Financially, we did very well. And-- but I was never happy. I didn't want to be there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your contact to come to America? Who helped you get to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Selma had an uncle who traveled very often to America. And he knew somebody in Buffalo, a lawyer. And he told him our story. And the man said, well, I really don't help people to go away from Israel, but I will do it-- do it for you. So he gave us-- I don't know what they call it-- a certificate or whatever the name is of it. And through his-- through his certificate-- let's call it certificate, maybe another word for it-- so that we could come to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, this is 1957?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '57. So we planned, really, to go-- first, she had brothers. She had a brother in Toronto to go-- because we didn't have any contact here in the States. So we couldn't, because if you immigrate, you have first to be here, then you can go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we didn't know what to do. There was a family from the place where we lived in Israel. Well, they went a year before us to Westport. Westport? Westport.

speaker: interviewer
text: Westport, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Westport. We were not really friends, but we knew each other. So we had written to them and tell them the story. She said, no problem, come to us. So we came first to Westport. That's when we end up in Westport.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after Westport, what did you do for a living when you came to the States?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came-- he worked for Gristedes as a clerk. So he found me a job. We had some money with us. Well, of course, we could rent an apartment. And we had some money, figuring one day might start something. Not much, but, we had-- maybe, for that time was much.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, so I got a job at Gristedes. And my job was to take telephone orders, telephone orders from customers. Now, I didn't hardly any-- very little English, and also didn't know what the items are. So I had a very hard time. But somehow, I struggled through, struggled through.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, it was-- there was-- it came by Gristedes, there came like the bread, Arnold Bread, you know. They had franchise. That was not direct from the company. You buy the franchise. You buy the truck and the distribution.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a route?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a route. And after seven or eight months, whatever, I bought a route like that, which was very good. And for three years-- also with the plan to go ahead and do something else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think three years I was in that to make some money, a good living, some money. And I was looking already for other things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now when did you move to Branford, where you are today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, let me tell you. It comes up.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I sold my route, and I bought a gift store in Stanford. And it was in the development that was really not so good. But I figured, well, I think I know business, not to brag about yourself. I was so successful in business, I had confidence in myself. And I bought this business. And we made a good living. We didn't make much money, but a good living. But I always was looking. I really was not yet where I wanted to be. So I was constantly looking, maybe a bigger card shop, a gift store and thing. But then it once came up. Our daughter was married to somebody, which was a manager in a jewelry store in Old Saybrook. And this store was for sale. And I had a buyer for my greeting card gift store. So we sold that. And I figured, he knows the jewelry business, and we can financially help it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we bought the business, and we took him as a partner, an equal partner. And we bought this jewelry business. And then we moved to Branford, because it was so far away to travel. So that's the reason we ended up-- we wanted to be-- we didn't move to Saybrook because we wanted to be next to a bigger city. Because when we lived in Westport, we went often to New York. So we figured New Haven is a bigger city. That's the reason we moved here. Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there is--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your daughters are all grown-- your daughter and son are all grown up now.

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter-- yeah. My son-- my daughter, she is in New Haven.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have two granddaughters from her.

speaker: interviewee
text: From her. And our son is in-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] in Northborough.

speaker: interviewer
text: And if you were-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] Chaim, if I were to ask you-- I know you and Selma speak in a lot of schools. [BACKGROUND NOISE] If you had a message that you wanted to give--

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you break it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Chaim, I know you and Selma go to a lot of schools, particularly in Connecticut, talking about the Sobibor uprising. But if you were to leave a message for the children of America, for your grandchildren, about what you learned as a survivor, what would that message be?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my message will be to be more open to people and not be hatred, understanding each other. Because the distinction between people is not big. It is people make more out of by themselves. So if you don't hate, you're not a bigot, and you're open to hear what the other says, you find out they're just the same as everybody else, whether it's Jews or not Jews or black or whatever. So I think that is our message, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure. Do you think it's important for the schoolchildren today to know there was a Sobibor and--

speaker: interviewee
text: I would think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was an uprising.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so because you learn only from the history, from the past. So you have to tell what happened, the people what bigotry and hatred can do. It you can kill a whole nation. So I think it's very important. We never know to who it happen, never know when it happened. So it is important to learn from history.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were a part of history by being part of Sobibor?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is there anything special you'd like to leave as a legacy to your grandchildren about your experiences, about surviving?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to know our story. They will know our story. I don't think it works as that they have to talk about at each generation-- we talk about is still fresh history. And the same thing we're telling the kids in school, it is still survivors, what they saw and they lived through, and they tell the story. And that is the history lesson.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the next generation, when the older generation is gone, it will be a history, like history of other stories, similar ones, what happened to Jews, the wars. But it happened to other people, too. They killed all the Armenians and the-- and the-- what is the name from Hungary? Oh, the Armenians were killed by the Turks.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the-- [INAUDIBLE]. Well, anyway. So it happened to a lot of people. And it can happen to everybody, especially minority. To tolerate people, to try to understand it, people are all people. There is not a nation which is only good, and there is not a nation which is only bad. You have good people with each nation, bad people in each nation. You have to judge the person by the person, not by the stereotype to say he's this, he's that. That gives a bad feeling. I mean, that causes all the trouble. You see what it started in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's very good advice. Thank you very much, Chaim.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.
